What Is a Port? Why Does Everyone Use 3000? A Practical Guide to Picking Ports
Why Does Everyone Use Port 3000? A Practical Guide to Picking Ports

A port is a number used to tell different services on the same machine apart. One computer only has one IP but can be running multiple services (Web, Database, FTP, etc.), so the port number is how the client knows which service to connect to. 3000 isn't special — it's just a Node.js community convention.

Students keep asking me: "What's a port? Why does everyone use 3000?" Rather than answering this one-on-one, here's the full version — what a port is, how to pick one, how to check if it's taken, and how to avoid the common gotchas.
— is something you've definitely seen as a developer:

So why do we need ports? Every machine that exposes services to the internet has an IP address, but the IP alone doesn't tell you what services are running on that machine. A single computer can be running several at the same time:
If all of these tried to share the same IP without anything to distinguish them, the client would have no idea which service to talk to. That's where the port comes in.
, the full connection strings would look like:
So that's what a port is — a way to tell services on the same machine apart.
How do I pick a port?
"How do I know which port to use?"
First, you need to know that ports range from 0 to 65535, and they split into three categories:

Are those ranges arbitrary? No — they're defined by IANA (the Internet Assigned Numbers Authority). Every well-known and registered port has a specific assigned purpose. If you're not sure about a port, you can check
to see whether it's already taken.
For example, you can look up Vite's port — Vite defaults to
, a port many web servers default to:
is technically registered for a piece of software called "RemoteWare Client RemoteWare", but there's a note: "
This entry records an unassigned but widespread use
". Translation: yes, the port has a registration on paper, but it's not really used in practice, so you can safely use
without worrying about clashing with the official service.
So when you're not sure what port to set, check IANA first. If the port you want isn't actually being used widely, go ahead — you won't be stepping on any real standard.
How do I check if a port is taken?

A port is how your machine tells network services apart — and 3000 is just a safe, unreserved default that dev tools standardised on. This post covers what ports are, how the reserved ranges work, how to pick one for your project, and how to find out what's hogging a port.
